authorship_evidence: Simple intentionally vulnerable demo app. Code style and comments are straightforward. Repository created for security testing/training purposes.

evidence.description: User-supplied 'ip' parameter from request.args is passed directly as an argument to subprocess.check_output(['ping', '-c', '4', ip]) without any validation or sanitization. While the list form prevents shell metacharacter injection, unsanitized input to a system command enables argument injection (e.g., passing flags like '-c 10000') and DNS-based attacks via attacker-controlled hostnames. Most SAST scanners will flag this as command injection.

Username ('admin') and password ('password123') are hardcoded as module-level constants USERNAME and PASSWORD, then used directly in the /login authentication check. Any attacker with source code access obtains valid credentials.

Untrusted data from request.data is passed directly to pickle.loads() at line 33. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve arbitrary code execution on the server. The /unserialize endpoint accepts raw POST body and deserializes it without any validation.

User-supplied 'id' parameter from request.args is concatenated directly into a SQL query string: "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '" + user_id + "'". This allows classic SQL injection. The query is then passed to run_query() which simulates database execution.

Flask application is run with debug=True (app.run(debug=True)). This enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger which exposes a Python REPL in the browser on unhandled exceptions, allowing arbitrary code execution. It also leaks detailed stack traces, source code, and environment variables to any user.

The /login endpoint at lines 19-26 accepts POST authentication requests with no rate limiting, account lockout, or brute-force protection. This is distinct from the hardcoded credentials finding (CWE-798 at lines 16-17) as it enables network-based brute-force attacks independently of source code access. An attacker can send unlimited authentication attempts with no throttle, lockout, or delay applied at any point in the request handling.

Reflected XSS in the get_user function. The user-supplied 'id' parameter from request.args is concatenated into a SQL query string, passed to run_query() which returns 'Query result for: ' + query (embedding the unsanitized user input), and the result is returned via 'return str(result)'. Flask serves this with text/html content type by default, so an attacker can inject HTML/JavaScript via the id parameter (e.g., id=<script>alert(1)</script>) which will be rendered in the victim's browser. This is distinct from the SQL injection vulnerability (GT-004) at line 46 — both share the same unsanitized input but target different sinks (browser HTML rendering vs database query).

The requests.get() call uses a hardcoded URL ('https://example.com'), not user-controlled input. There is no SSRF vulnerability because the target URL cannot be influenced by an attacker. Scanners may flag the use of requests.get() but the destination is a static constant.

The run_query() function looks like it executes a SQL query but is actually a simulation that only performs string concatenation and returns the result as text. No actual database connection or query execution occurs — it simply returns 'Query result for: ' + query. The real SQL injection vulnerability is at line 46 where the query string is constructed with unsanitized input.
